data.title: Pembina Pipeline reaches settlement with shippers on Alliance Pipeline
data.description:
Pembina Pipeline (NYSE:PBA) +1.2% post-market Thursday after announcing a negotiated settlement with shippers on the Canadian portion of the Alliance Pipeline and filed for regulatory approval with the Canada Energy Regulator.

The 10-year settlement, effective November 1, includes revised term-differentiated tolls that are expected to reduce existing long-term firm tolls by an average of 14% on a volume weighted average basis, the company said.

The agreement, which follows a November 2024 order from the CER to submit a detailed toll application, was supported by a Shipper Committee consisting of more than 30 members in a vote on July 23.

Pembina (NYSE:PBA) said it expects the financial impact to Alliance from the settlement over the next 10 years will be a ~$50M/year reduction in long-term firm service revenue, plus the impact of the new revenue sharing provision.

Separately, Alliance said it is soliciting non-binding expressions of interest for a prospective regional short-haul expansion providing transportation capacity to Alberta's industrial heartland; as part of the proposed offering, Alliance has initiated a feasibility study for up to 350M cf/day of incremental capacity with an anticipated in-service date in late 2029.

data.title: Deckers signals $1.38B–$1.42B Q2 revenue target amid robust HOKA and UGG growth, while navigating tariff headwinds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Deckers Outdoor Corporation (DECK) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Stefano Caroti stated fiscal year 2026 began with HOKA and UGG both outperforming expectations, highlighting "revenue growing 17% versus last year to $965 million and diluted earnings per share increasing 24% to $0.93." He emphasized "HOKA delivered its largest quarter in its history," with significant international momentum and strong sell-through during key model transitions.
  • Caroti reported "HOKA performance came in ahead of our expectations," with global wholesale up 30% and DTC increasing 3% globally, citing EMEA and APAC regions as key growth drivers. U.S. performance was "aligned with our expectations." He described ongoing expansion into international markets and selective retail store growth, particularly in China and Europe.
  • The CEO outlined new execution initiatives for HOKA, including "adjusting product life cycles," "tightening marketplace inventory targets on outgoing models," and enhancing the DTC loyalty program. He noted "the consumer is showing a strong affinity for the updates to the HOKA brand's three largest franchises."
  • On UGG, Caroti stated global revenue increased 19% to $265 million, led by a 30% jump in wholesale and strong international growth, particularly in EMEA and China. The success of UGG’s PeakMod and 365 initiatives in sandal and sneaker segments was highlighted.
  • CFO Steven J. Fasching said, "Deckers again delivered another quarter of strong results that came in above our expectations for the first quarter." He detailed "Total company revenue was $965 million, up 17% versus the prior year," and explained gross margin was 55.8%, down from 56.9% last year due to "unfavorable channel mix with wholesale growing faster than DTC, increased promotion across UGG and HOKA... and higher freight rates."

Outlook

  • The company is not providing a formal full-year outlook for fiscal year 2026, citing "continued macroeconomic uncertainty related to the global trade policy and difficulty predicting impact on the consumer environment and purchasing behavior."
  • Fasching reiterated, "our fiscal year 2026 framework includes the following: from a revenue perspective, we expect HOKA to continue as our fastest-growing brand, UGG continuing to grow, international to outpace U.S. growth and wholesale to outpace DTC in the near term."
  • For Q2 fiscal 2026, Deckers expects revenue in the range of $1.38 billion to $1.42 billion, with "HOKA increasing approximately 10%... and UGG increasing at least mid-single digits." Gross margin is expected to be between 53.5% and 54%, with diluted EPS in the range of $1.50 to $1.55 compared to last year's $1.59.
  • Fasching noted, "we would expect to face a total of $185 million of unmitigated impact to our cost of goods sold in fiscal year 2026, up from our previously provided estimate of up to $150 million," due to increased tariffs.

Financial Results

  • Deckers reported $965 million in revenue for Q1 2026, a 17% increase year-over-year. HOKA contributed $653 million and UGG $265 million, with international revenue up 50%.
  • Gross margin for the quarter was 55.8%, down 110 basis points from last year, attributed to "unfavorable channel mix," increased promotional activity, and higher freight, partially offset by favorable product mix and currency effects.
  • SG&A for the quarter was $373 million, 38.6% of revenue, with growth driven by increased marketing, logistics, and rent costs due to store expansion. Tax rate increased to 24% from 22.5% last year.
  • Inventory was $849 million (up 13% year-over-year), and Deckers ended June 2025 with $1.7 billion in cash and equivalents, with no outstanding borrowings. The company repurchased $183 million in shares during the quarter.

Q&A

  • Jay Daniel Sole, UBS: Asked about HOKA’s Q2 guidance and the wholesale vs. DTC channel performance, as well as inventory status and product pipeline. Fasching explained the "10% growth" reflects some earlier wholesale shipments and expects "a little bit more balanced growth between the two channels." Caroti indicated the market is "largely clean of Bondi 8s and Clifton 9s... Arahi 8... is performing very well."
  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas: Questioned DTC growth, store expansion, and the impact of tariffs on order books. Fasching said improvements are expected in U.S. DTC performance and noted "the framework still holds" for mid-teens HOKA growth and mid-single digit UGG growth, pending tariff clarity.
  • Adrienne Yih-Tennant, Barclays: Inquired about the price increase strategy and gross margin outlook. Caroti said price increases are "selective and staggered." Fasching added that more margin pressure is expected in the back half of fiscal 2026 due to tariffs.
  • Jonathan Komp, Baird: Sought further detail on HOKA innovation and cash usage for buybacks. Caroti highlighted investments in "innovation, in design, in color and engineering," with upcoming launches. Fasching stated strong cash supports share repurchases.
  • Rick Patel, Raymond James: Asked about HOKA's international growth drivers and SG&A outlook. Caroti pointed out "revenue growth is outpacing door expansion and sell-through continues to outpace sell-in." Fasching emphasized continued "well-managed" investments in global brand building.
  • Samuel Poser, Williams Trading: Probed retail vs. e-commerce performance and promotional activity. Fasching said retail stores "performed better than e-commerce significantly." Caroti confirmed "no material decline in performance for the products that we have raised price on."
  • John David Kernan, TD Cowen: Asked about U.S. vs. international growth and HOKA DTC inflection. Fasching expects "incremental improvement in Q2" for both regions and noted DTC is "encouraged" but expects gradual gains rather than dramatic shifts.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism but focused questioning on tariff impacts, DTC performance, and gross margin headwinds. The tone was pressing, especially on topics of inventory, pricing strategy, and channel mix.
  • Management maintained a confident but measured tone, frequently reiterating brand strength and proactive initiatives. Phrases like "we're confident," "we believe," and "we continue to be excited" were used, with some defensiveness in discussing gross margin and promotional activity.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management sustained confidence, but analysts showed increased concern about tariffs and margin pressures. Management's prepared remarks were more upbeat, while their Q&A responses were more cautious and detailed.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remained cautious, with no formal full-year outlook due to "continued macroeconomic uncertainty," in line with last quarter's approach.
  • Strategic focus remains on international expansion, innovation investment, and balancing wholesale and DTC channels, consistent with prior quarter themes.
  • Analysts' questions shifted more heavily toward tariff impact, price execution, and DTC recovery, reflecting growing concerns over margin compression and consumer response to higher prices.
  • Key metrics such as revenue growth, international sales, and inventory levels all increased sequentially, but gross margin declined from the previous year.
  • Management’s confidence in their brands and operating model remained strong, but their commentary on macro risks and mitigation strategies became more granular.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted "macroeconomic uncertainty related to the global trade policy," specifically noting tariff-related cost headwinds now estimated at $185 million for fiscal 2026.
  • Gross margin pressure is expected to persist, driven by "increased tariffs, higher levels of promotion, upgraded materials on key styles and higher ocean freight rates."
  • Analysts raised concerns about DTC performance, inventory management, and the effectiveness of price increases and promotional strategies.
  • Management is implementing mitigation strategies, including selective price increases, tighter expense control, and continued evaluation of cost-sharing with suppliers.

Final Takeaway

Deckers Brands opened fiscal year 2026 with robust revenue and earnings growth, driven by record HOKA performance and strong international momentum for both HOKA and UGG. While facing heightened uncertainty from increased tariffs and macroeconomic pressures, management emphasized disciplined execution, strategic investments, and brand innovation as core strengths. The company remains confident in its ability to achieve long-term growth, supported by a solid balance sheet, proactive cost management, and continued consumer demand for its flagship brands.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Cadence Bank outlines 11%-15% loan growth and 10%-12% revenue growth targets for 2025 following recent acquisitions
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Cadence Bank (CADE) Q2 2025

Management View

  • Chairman & CEO James Rollins highlighted an active quarter for Cadence, stating, "On the M&A front, we announced our acquisition of Industry Bancshares on April 25. We then completed our acquisition of First Chatham Bank effective May 1, and we closed the industry transaction on July 1.... We are excited about the opportunity to expand our presence in Georgia and Central Texas." He also noted, "Adjusted net income from continuing operations increased to $137.5 million or $0.73 per share, and adjusted ROA was 1.14% for the quarter." Rollins emphasized organic loan growth of $1.1 billion for the quarter, describing it as "12.6% annualized," and cited strong pipelines across business lines. He added, "Our core customer deposit balances also showed growth, which offset some intentional runoff in brokered and a seasonal decline in public fund balances."
  • CFO Valerie Toalson stated, "Our pretax pre-provision net revenue for the second quarter increased to an all-time high of $206 million, up over 8% from the prior quarter... Average loans were up a little over $800 million in the quarter, while period-end loans grew by $1.4 billion, a $1.1 billion in organic growth and close to $400 million from the First Chatham acquisition." Toalson also explained, "Net interest revenue increased $15 million or 4% as a result of the robust loan growth as well as added securities... The NIM declined 6 basis points in the second quarter to 3.40%." She highlighted, "Loan yields were 6.34% in the quarter, up 1 basis point from the first quarter, and new and renewed loans in the quarter came on the books at just over 7%."

Outlook

  • Cadence Bank updated guidance to reflect both the First Chatham and Industry acquisitions. Toalson reported, "We continue to expect solid loan demand for the latter half of the year, bringing full year loan growth, including the acquisitions to between 11% and 15%. This, combined with full year core customer deposit growth of between 12% and 15%, supports our expectation for total revenue growth between 10% and 12%." She further stated, "We forecast continued operating leverage with expenses increasing between 7% and 9% in support of the growth in the balance sheet and continued investment in our future. Combined with stable credit, we expect these results will continue to drive strong EPS performance for us throughout the rest of this year."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted net income from continuing operations reached $137.5 million or $0.73 per share. Pretax pre-provision net revenue was $206 million, described as an "all-time high." Total adjusted revenue was $476 million, an increase of $28 million or 6%. Net interest revenue was up $15 million or 4% from the prior quarter. Organic loan growth was $1.1 billion, with period-end loans up by $1.4 billion including acquisitions. Organic core customer deposits increased at a 4.4% annualized rate. Noninterest-bearing deposits as a percentage of total deposits increased to 22.6%. Net charge-offs for the quarter were $21 million or 24 basis points annualized. Tangible book value increased to $22.94 per share, and CET1 capital stood at 12.2%.
  • Toalson noted, "Adjusted noninterest revenue reflected great performance really across the board, increasing $13 million or 15% compared to the first quarter."

Q&A

  • Casey Haire, Autonomous Research: Asked about net interest margin (NIM) rebound potential and further restructuring. Rollins answered, "On the industry piece, we've done what we wanted to do... But right now, that's not on the top burn." Toalson added, "We expect some continued improvement there. So we're actually very optimistic about the net interest margin and anticipate that, that would increase as we go through the rest of the year."
  • Manan Gosalia, Morgan Stanley: Inquired about updated revenue guidance and purchase accounting assumptions. Rollins replied, "With the increased guide on loan growth, that's going to produce organic revenue growth that you're seeing in the increased guide there." Toalson said, "Our interest rate or the securities... we assumed a 2.5% liquidity mark... we're able to dispose of those securities at less than half of that. So we're coming in much better on that front."
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: Queried about new origination volumes versus paydown activity and changes in accretion. Fisackerly explained, "In the first quarter, we saw a lot in the merchant real estate portfolio from a paydown standpoint and from our midstream energy. We saw that kind of slow, while at the same time, continuing really good origination." Toalson clarified, "The accretion would be a little bit less. But of course, your upfront impact to your tangible book value is less."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Asked about core deposit growth and CD renewal rates. Toalson responded, "We had about $3.8 billion in originations of CDs that were at just shy of 360... there's an opportunity to continue to see a little bit of compression in that overall amount."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on growth sustainability, margin rebound, and integration of acquisitions, with a generally positive tone but pressing for clarity on guidance details and acquisition impacts.
  • Management maintained a confident outlook, repeatedly emphasizing strong pipelines, execution of integration, and positive expectations for NIM and revenue, as evidenced by Toalson's statement, "We are actually very optimistic about the net interest margin and anticipate that, that would increase as we go through the rest of the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management's tone was more assertive regarding growth prospects, while analysts shifted from cautious optimism to more direct inquiries about execution and forward guidance.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance was updated to higher full-year growth ranges for loans and deposits (now 11%-15% and 12%-15% respectively, compared to steady guidance previously).
  • Strategic focus shifted toward integration and immediate contribution of recent acquisitions, with senior management highlighting rapid M&A execution.
  • Key metrics improved quarter-over-quarter, including net income, revenue, and loan growth, whereas in Q1, management had highlighted stable but less pronounced growth.
  • Analysts’ questions this quarter concentrated more on the sustainability of improved performance and integration impacts, compared to the prior quarter’s focus on macroeconomic uncertainty and margin stability.
  • Management exuded greater confidence in achieving targets, frequently referencing robust pipelines and successful acquisition integrations, while in Q1, the tone was more cautious given broader economic uncertainties.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged increased legal costs due to "final resolution of a legal matter."
  • Slight rise in criticized and classified loans linked to "a handful of credits," but balances remain within historical ranges.
  • Credit trends described as stable, with net charge-offs and nonperforming asset levels consistent with expectations.
  • Analysts expressed concerns about deposit cost pressures, integration risks, and the durability of strong loan pipelines, to which management responded by underscoring balance sheet strength and ongoing monitoring.

Final Takeaway

Cadence Bank’s management underscored a quarter marked by record loan growth, robust earnings, and rapid integration of two acquisitions, while raising full-year targets for loan, deposit, and revenue growth. Management expressed optimism regarding margin improvement and continued operating leverage, and emphasized that successful integration combined with strong pipelines positions the company for continued EPS performance through 2025 and into 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: First Merchants outlines sustained loan growth and efficiency amid robust Midwest expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: First Merchants Corporation (FRME) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Mark Hardwick opened the call by stating, "We delivered 9.1% annualized loan growth and $0.98 of earnings per share. Return on assets totaled 1.23% and our efficiency ratio was 54%, which is consistent with the high-performance company we strive to be." He emphasized a net income of $56.4 million for the quarter, a 43% increase year-over-year, attributing the improvement to credit quality normalization and a significantly lower provision for credit losses.
  • Hardwick highlighted, "We also repurchased an additional $22.1 million worth of shares this quarter. And year-to-date, we've repurchased $31.7 million with an average price of $38.68. Our tangible common equity of 8.92% is above our target level and provides optimal capital flexibility."
  • President Michael Stewart stated, "$262 million of commercial loan growth for the quarter, that's over 10% annualized. $430 million of loan growth year-to-date, that's 9% annualized." He cited CapEx financing, increased revolver usage, M&A financings, and new business conversions as growth drivers, with commercial deposits also increasing significantly.
  • CFO Michele Kawiecki reported, "Total revenues in Q2 were strong with meaningful growth in both net interest income of $2.7 million and noninterest income of $1.3 million." She added, "Pretax pre-provision earnings of $70.7 million" and noted, "Expenses declined, creating meaningful operating leverage. Pretax pre-provision earnings totaled $138.1 million, reflecting growth of 7.3%."
  • Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer John Martin commented, "In Q2, we saw balanced loan growth across the portfolio with a $298 million increase quarter-over-quarter or 9.2% annualized and $654 million year-over-year or 5.2%. C&I lending grew by $147 million this quarter. Our Midwest footprint remains core with 82.7% of borrowers located in our 4-state region."

Outlook

  • Management signaled confidence in continued loan growth, with Stewart noting a consistent quarter-end pipeline and optimism for maintaining loan growth and market share through the third quarter. Kawiecki stated, "I do think that, that will put some modest compression in margin in the back half of the year compared to this quarter" due to rising deposit costs and competitive market dynamics.
  • Kawiecki expects noninterest income to "grow into the mid-single digits in the back half of the year" and noted continued discipline in expense management, projecting "maybe 1% to 2%" expense increase in Q3 and Q4 related to incentive accruals and marketing.
  • The company continues to balance capital deployment, with Hardwick reiterating, "As our stock price works its way further north, I think we'll be less aggressive with buybacks," while keeping M&A opportunities in consideration.

Financial Results

  • Second quarter net income was $56.4 million, up $17 million from the prior year. Earnings per share reached $0.98. Year-to-date net income totaled $111.2 million, up $24.3 million or 28% over the first half of 2024, and year-to-date EPS was $1.92, an increase of $0.44 or 30%.
  • Net interest income was $139.2 million, an increase of $2.8 million from the prior quarter. Net interest margin was 3.25%, up 3 basis points. The total loan portfolio yield increased to 6.32%, with new originations and refis averaging 7.04%.
  • Noninterest income for the quarter was $31.3 million, with customer-related fees of $29.4 million and notable increases in gains on the sale of mortgages and treasury management fees. Noninterest expense totaled $93.6 million, a modest increase of $700,000 from the prior quarter.
  • Tangible book value rose by $2.80 to $27.90, up 11.2% year-over-year. The company’s tangible common equity ratio grew to 8.92%, and the common equity Tier 1 ratio was 11.35%.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about funding cost pressure and margin outlook. Kawiecki responded, "We did see deposit costs rise a little bit. And I do think that, that will put some modest compression in margin in the back half of the year compared to this quarter."
  • Tamayo also inquired about loan growth sustainability. Hardwick indicated, "I view it as core, working right through direct client growth in their plans, meeting their expectations."
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens: Questioned wealth management fees and fee income outlook. Kawiecki replied, "I would expect to see noninterest income grow into the mid-single digits in the back half of the year." Stewart added that private wealth and retirement plan services fees were relatively flat but expected to drive future fee income growth.
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about capital deployment and brokered funding thresholds. Hardwick stated, "We use 1/3 of our earnings to support balance sheet growth, about 1/3 for dividends and the other 1/3 for kind of other capital activities." Kawiecki noted, "Typically, we kind of have an internal threshold of about 10%" for brokered funding.
  • Nathan Race, Piper Sandler: Sought details on margin sensitivity to rate cuts and pipeline strength. Kawiecki explained, "With each rate cut on an annualized basis, I would expect about 2 basis points of compression for each 25 basis point rate cut."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Queried expense outlook and provision levels. Kawiecki projected a "very modest increase in expenses in Q3, Q4, I would say, maybe 1% to 2%." On provision, "presuming that the macroeconomic forecasts are pretty stable, I think we'll just continue to provide for the growth that we see in our balance sheet."
  • Brian Martin, Janney: Asked about deposit costs and fixed rate loan repricing. Kawiecki said, "It could" trend higher, depending on deposit competition, and noted about $200 million of fixed rate loans will reprice in the back half of 2025 at around 5%.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised concerns about funding costs, margin compression, and the competitive deposit landscape, with a slightly cautious tone, particularly around the margin and funding cost outlook.
  • Management maintained confidence, though acknowledged modest margin compression and ongoing competition. Kawiecki’s statement, "I do think that, that will put some modest compression in margin in the back half of the year compared to this quarter," reflected a realistic but reassuring stance.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from neutral to slightly negative regarding funding pressures, while management remained steady in confidence but offered more explicit caution about margin pressures.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Loan growth accelerated from 4.8% annualized in Q1 to 9.1% in Q2, with commercial and C&I lending as main drivers.
  • Net income and EPS rose more substantially in Q2 than Q1, with efficiency ratios and capital ratios also improving.
  • Deposit cost pressures increased, reversing the Q1 decline. Management’s tone shifted to address more competitive market dynamics, while analysts focused more on margin risks versus the prior quarter’s emphasis on loan growth and credit quality.
  • Expense management remained a highlight, but the outlook now includes minor increases tied to strategic hires and incentive accruals.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited rising deposit competition and funding cost pressures as current challenges, with the potential for modest margin compression if these dynamics persist.
  • Macro uncertainties, including rate cut impacts and ongoing competitive deposit specials, present risks to net interest margin stability.
  • Isolated nonaccruals in the loan portfolio were noted, but management described them as "isolated cases" that are being actively managed.
  • The company continues to monitor office CRE exposures and noted careful management of brokered funding to remain within internal thresholds.

Final Takeaway

First Merchants Corporation reported robust second quarter earnings, driven by accelerating loan growth, disciplined expense management, and strong credit quality. The company continues to focus on core commercial banking across its Midwest footprint, while acknowledging funding cost pressures and the likelihood of modest margin compression in the second half of the year. Strategic capital deployment, including share repurchases and selective hiring, remains central to sustaining growth and market share. Management expressed confidence in the company's ability to navigate a competitive environment, with a continued emphasis on organic growth and operational efficiency.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Colony Bankcorp outlines immediate EPS accretion and 1.19% ROA target through TC Bancshares merger
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Colony Bankcorp, Inc. (CBAN) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO T. Heath Fountain reported, "We're pleased to report improved financial performance this quarter, which reflects the continued improvement of our operations and success and discipline of our team members." Fountain highlighted core earnings improvement, loan growth at a 15% annualized rate, and net interest margin expansion, attributing success to pricing discipline and a stable core deposit base.
  • The company announced a definitive merger agreement with TC Bancshares, expanding into South Georgia and North Florida, which Fountain described as "a compelling strategic and cultural fit." He stated, "The combination is a transformational step that enhances our franchise and positions us for sustained long-term growth in key markets, both in Georgia and Florida."
  • Fountain also announced the addition of two bankers in the Chattanooga MSA, aiming to expand the company's presence in that market, and celebrated Colony’s 50th anniversary with a New York Stock Exchange bell ringing.
  • CFO Derek W. Shelnutt stated, "Net income increased $1.4 million compared to the first quarter. Increased net interest income and lower provision expense on improving credit metrics were the major contributing factors to the increase, along with improved noninterest revenue."
  • Shelnutt discussed noninterest income improvement, an increase of over $1 million, and highlighted gains in mortgage, SBSL, and service charge-related revenue. He also provided detail on noninterest expense increases, driven by higher variable-based compensation and a $340,000 SBA servicing asset valuation adjustment.
  • Gregory H. Eiford, CEO of TC Federal, said, "When evaluating strategic options...it became clear that Colony was the ideal partner. Their proven track record, forward-looking strategy and commitment to doing things the right way made them the right choice."

Outlook

  • Fountain projected loan growth to moderate to a range of 10% to 12% for the second half of the year, compared to 15% in the current quarter. He indicated the company is confident in maintaining a 1% or better return on assets (ROA) and is now targeting an intermediate goal of 1.2% ROA.
  • Shelnutt said, "We expect margin to continue to increase going forward, and it will likely be more moderate compared to this past quarter."
  • The merger with TC Bancshares is expected to be immediately accretive to EPS, excluding one-time costs, and to drive "double-digit EPS accretion by year 2." Shelnutt shared, "We expect approximately 8.4% EPS accretion in 2026 and 11.9% in 2027."

Financial Results

  • Net income grew by $1.4 million quarter-over-quarter, driven by higher net interest income and lower provision expense. Net interest income rose by approximately $1.4 million, with cost of funds down 3 basis points to 2.04% and net interest margin up 19 basis points to 3.12%.
  • Noninterest income increased by over $1 million, with noted strength in mortgage, SBSL, and service charges. Noninterest expenses rose by $1.8 million due to variable-based compensation and data processing linked to higher activity.
  • Loans held for investment increased by $72.3 million. The weighted average new and renewed loan rate for the quarter was 7.78%.
  • Total deposits decreased by $66 million, which management attributed to seasonal runoff, but core customer deposits were up year-over-year by more than $75 million.
  • The insurance division saw policy sales increase by 50% from March to June, though pretax income was flat compared to the previous quarter.
  • The company repurchased 62,000 shares at an average price of $15.46 and declared a quarterly cash dividend of $0.115 per share.

Q&A

  • Christopher William Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Asked for an update on organic loan growth and deposit costs. Fountain responded that loan growth would likely be 10% to 12% for the rest of the year and deposit costs are expected to remain flat barring Fed action.
  • Marinac: Inquired about EPS accretion timing from the TC merger. Fountain noted it is based both on system conversion timing and continued organic growth, with no exact conversion date set yet.
  • Marinac: Asked about further acquisition prospects post-TC merger. Fountain stated, "We continue to look for opportunities. We continue to have conversations...but that is the #1 priority is making sure this goes through well."
  • Unidentified Analyst, Hovde Group: Sought insight on the health of the loan portfolio, especially the SBA segment. Fountain explained that nonperforming, criticized, and classified levels declined, and charge-offs were mainly in older SBSL loans. He expects elevated charge-offs to persist in this area, but premium revenue remains strong.
  • Unidentified Analyst: Asked about runway for loan repricing. Fountain said, "We still are in a really good place in terms of the ability to see assets continue to reprice...Our overall portfolio yield is only slightly above 6%."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts posed questions focused on sustainability of growth, merger integration, and asset quality, showing a neutral to slightly positive tone as they probed for clarity on forward guidance and merger benefits.
  • Management maintained a confident tone, especially regarding merger benefits and operational performance, using phrases such as "We're pleased with our performance this quarter, and we're very excited about the partnership with TC."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased confidence, with management showing greater optimism about margin improvement, merger synergies, and market expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance shifted from expecting normalized loan growth later in the year to achieving higher loan growth earlier and then moderating to 10%-12% for the remainder of the year.
  • Strategic focus moved from integration of Ellerbee Agency and new credit card offerings in Q1 to the transformational merger with TC Bancshares in Q2.
  • Key metrics showed improvement in ROA, net interest income, and margin, with the Q2 ROA reaching 1.02%—ahead of prior projections.
  • Management's tone became more assertive regarding growth prospects, merger benefits, and operational execution, while analysts maintained a consistent focus on core performance and integration risks.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged some increase in net charge-offs, primarily in the SBSL division, related to older loans, and expects this to remain somewhat elevated.
  • Seasonal deposit runoff led to a $66 million decline in deposits, but management expects deposits to return in late Q3 and Q4.
  • Integration risk from the TC Bancshares merger was noted, but management emphasized cultural alignment and operational experience to mitigate challenges.

Final Takeaway

Colony Bankcorp highlighted improved core earnings, expanding margins, and robust loan growth, while announcing a strategic merger with TC Bancshares designed to deliver immediate EPS accretion and significant long-term growth opportunities. Management remains confident in sustaining a 1% or better ROA, with the merger expected to drive performance enhancements, broaden market reach, and strengthen the combined balance sheet, positioning the company as a leading community bank in the Southeast.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Paramount gains as FCC approves Skydance merger
data.description:
Paramount Global stock rose after hours Thursday -- (NASDAQ:PARA) +2.2%, (NASDAQ:PARAA) +2.1% -- after the Federal Communications Commission approved the company's acquisition by Skydance.

The move removes the last hurdle in a months-long takeover saga that wrapped up the CBS news division and its flagship newsmagazine 60 Minutes, in a lawsuit filed by President Trump ultimately settled for millions of dollars.

The agency signed off on the deal by way of granting a set of applications transferring FCC licenses and authorizations. The approval followed a set of promises made by Skydance's David Ellison to the administration about news practices at the new Paramount.

"Americans no longer trust the legacy national news media to report fully, accurately, and fairly. It is time for a change," FCC Chairman Brendan Carr said in a statement accompanying the decision. "That is why I welcome Skydance’s commitment to make significant changes at the once storied CBS broadcast network."

"In particular, Skydance has made written commitments to ensure that the new company’s programming embodies a diversity of viewpoints from across the political and ideological spectrum. Skydance will also adopt measures that can root out the bias that has undermined trust in the national news media," Carr said.

Among the promises made to secure the approval, Skydance has committed to "fair, unbiased and fact-based" reporting at CBS; an ombudsman who will evaluate complaints of bias for at least two years; that Skydance won't establish diversity, equity and inclusion policies at New Paramount; and that it will commit to local news as a core component of the FCC's public interest standard.

data.title: Newmont routs Q2 estimates on surging gold prices, lower mining costs
data.description:
Newmont (NYSE:NEM) +4.1% post-market Thursday after reporting higher Q2 profit from a year ago that also easily beat analyst expectations, as the world's top publicly-traded gold miner benefited from the rally in bullion prices in recent months.

Q2 net income surged to $2.06B, or $1.85/share, from $838M, or $0.74/share, in the year-earlier quarter, and revenues jumped 21% Y/Y to $5.32B from $4.4B a year ago, as the company said its average realized gold price for the period jumped 41% to $3,320/oz from $2,347/oz in the same quarter of 2024.

The gold price rally helped cushion a 4% decline in Newmont's (NYSE:NEM) attributable gold production to 1.48M oz in the quarter, after the company began divesting non-core assets last year to reduce debt in the months following the completion of its $17.1B acquisition of Newcrest Mining.

Newmont (NEM) said Q2 all-in-sustaining costs for gold of $1,593/oz rose nearly 2% Y/Y but fell 4% Q/Q, citing lower spending across its operations, particularly at two of its Australian mines and its Lihir mine in Papua New Guinea.

The drop in costs marks a sharp turnaround for the company, which in Q1 posted its highest quarterly costs in at least nine years, limiting its ability to fully take advantage of the sharp rise in the bullion market.

The company also said it would begin a $3B stock buyback program and said it remains on track to meet its previously issued full-year production guidance of 5.9M oz.

data.title: Pinnacle Financial, Synovus to combine in deal value at $8.6B
data.description:
Pinnacle Financial (NASDAQ:PNFP) and Synovus FInancial (NYSE:SNV) announced an agreement to combine in all-stock transaction valued at $8.6 billion. Shares of Synovus fell 9.2% in after hours trading, while Pinnacle dropped 5%.

Under the terms of the agreement, the shares of Synovus (NYSE:SNV) and Pinnacle (NASDAQ:PNFP) holders will be converted into shares of a new Pinnacle parent company based on a fixed exchange ratio of 0.5237 Synovus shares per Pinnacle share, according to a statement on Thursday. The exchange ratio represents a per share value of $61.18, a 10% premium to Synovu's unaffected share price.

Synovus shareholders will own approximately 48.5% and Pinnacle shareholders will own approximately 51.5% of the combined company

The combined company will operate under the Pinancle Financials Partners and Pinnacle Bank name and brand.  Kevin Blair, Chairman, Chief Executive Officer and President of Synovus, will serve as President and Chief Executive Officer of the combined company, and Terry Turner, President and Chief Executive Officer of Pinnacle, will serve as Chairman of the board of the combined company.

The transaction is expected to close in the first quarter of 2026.

The deal announcement comes after Bloomberg reported on Tuesday that Synovus (SNV) was evaluating options, including a merger, after receiving interest from a rival bank.

The deal is expected to be about 21% accretive to Pinnacle’s (PNFP) estimated operating earnings per share in 2027, according to the statement.

data.title: Icon signals $8B full-year revenue midpoint as metabolic and obesity trial momentum builds
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ICON Public Limited Company (ICLR) Q2 2025

Management View

  • CEO Steven A. Cutler highlighted an 11% sequential increase in gross business awards over quarter 1, driven by wins from biotech customers and ongoing expansion in large pharma partnerships added in the last 18 months.
  • Revenue performance was described as "ahead of expectations, assisted by higher pass-through revenue in the quarter," with a noted burn rate of 8.2% in Q2. Cutler stated, "Gross margin improved over quarter 1 to 28.3% and SG&A costs reduced by $9 million year-over-year."
  • Cutler acknowledged "elevated cancellations," attributing the rise to the loss of a large next-generation COVID vaccine trial and a broad mix of portfolio rationalization and negative clinical trial results. He pointed to "encouraging level of actionable opportunities in the pipeline" for the second half of the year and noted increased M&A and licensing activity among large pharma companies as a positive sign for ICON’s strategic positioning.
  • The company launched its Center for Obesity in Q2, establishing a U.S. network of over 100 sites targeting rapid site activation and access to over 10,000 prescreened patients. The majority of these sites operate on an integrated technology platform, which "allows for improved efficiencies in processes across enrollment and recruitment as well as in real-time monitoring."
  • Cutler also described "meaningful applied advances" in digital innovation, particularly with a new AI-driven protocol digitization tool that "enables ICON to achieve upper quartile performance metrics for our sponsors, allowing significantly reduced study start-up times and improved overall project time lines."
  • The Board approved a new $1 billion share repurchase authorization, an increase of $500 million from the prior authorization.
  • CFO Nigel Clerkin reported, "Revenue in quarter 2 was $2.017 billion, representing a year-on-year decrease of 4.8%. Revenue was up approximately 1% sequentially on quarter 1 2025." Clerkin also noted adjusted EBITDA was $396 million for the quarter and highlighted share repurchases of $250 million.

Outlook

  • Full-year revenue guidance was updated to a new range of $7.85 billion at the low end and $8.15 billion at the high end, "increasing the midpoint to $8 billion." The full-year adjusted earnings per share guidance midpoint remained at $13.50.
  • Cutler stated, "We remain confident in the prudent approach we took in setting our full year outlook in April and have kept our assumptions consistent regarding macro conditions through the balance of the year."
  • Management expects "book-to-bill at roughly the same level through the rest of the year" and burn rate "to be broadly stable over the balance of the year and somewhere around 8% for the full year."

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q2 was $2.017 billion, a decrease from the same quarter last year but an increase of about 1% sequentially.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $396 million, with a margin of 19.6%, and adjusted earnings per share was $3.26. Adjusted SG&A expense was $174.8 million, 8.7% of revenue.
  • U.S. GAAP net income in Q2 was $183 million, or $2.30 per diluted share. Cash from operating activities was $146.2 million, with free cash flow of $113.9 million, and the quarter ended with $390.4 million in cash and $3.4 billion in debt.
  • The company continued to repurchase shares, totaling $250 million in Q2.

Q&A

  • Elizabeth Hammell Anderson, Evercore ISI: Asked about demand trends between biotech and pharma. Cutler responded, "There's a modest uptick... probably been more in the Biotech segment than it has been in the large Pharma segment... a reasonably constructive development sort of environment, if you like, across the business, probably a little bit more in biotech than in large pharma."
  • Michael Cherny, Leerink Partners: Queried improvement in biotech bookings despite funding headwinds. Cutler replied, "Within the quarters and across the quarters, it has been a little bit more volatile, and we still continue to see caution... 3 of the top 4 awards that we had during the quarter were in the biotech segment."
  • Patrick Donnelly, Citi: Asked about the sustainability of positive bookings trends. Cutler said, "We feel constructive on the environment and moving forward... But it remains to be seen as to whether those opportunities... continue. Obviously, I've mentioned the cancellations will continue to be elevated, certainly in the very short term."
  • Jack Meehan, Nephron Research: Inquired about market share dynamics. Cutler said, "I sense that we are being successful in moving our market share forward, but it's hard to be too quantitative on that... Our FSP business has -- continues to grow. We've made nice progress in our early phase business. Our lab business is growing in the teens."
  • Charles Rhyee, TD Cowen: Sought clarification on cancellations and book-to-bill. Cutler stated, "We're expecting to see a -- in the same sort of post code as what we saw this quarter from a -- the fact that we did call out the BARDA cancel earlier on... But I think certainly, but the very near term, that's the expectation."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts raised recurring concerns about the volatility of biotech funding, sustainability of improved bookings, competitive pricing, and elevated cancellations. The tone was cautiously optimistic but included skepticism regarding the disconnect between channel checks and reported biotech momentum.
  • Management maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone in prepared remarks, shifting to a more cautious and measured approach during Q&A, especially when addressing competition and cancellation trends. Cutler frequently used phrases such as "constructive on the market" and "not declaring victory just at this point," signaling measured optimism.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remain concerned about cancellations and bookings, while management showed a slight increase in confidence but continued to emphasize volatility and uncertainty.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Gross business awards and revenue showed sequential improvement, while year-over-year comparisons remained negative for both revenue and earnings per share.
  • The company increased its full-year revenue guidance midpoint from Q1, while holding adjusted EPS midpoint steady, reflecting increased pass-through revenue as the primary driver of the guidance change.
  • Management’s tone shifted from "cautious optimism" in Q1 to a more "constructive" but still careful outlook in Q2, with continued acknowledgment of headwinds from study delays and cancellations.
  • Analysts’ focus remained on cancellations, pricing pressure, and the sustainability of positive trends in biotech and metabolic trial opportunities.
  • Strategic priorities evolved to emphasize metabolic and obesity trials, digital innovation, and expanded partnerships, especially in biotech and midsized pharma.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited "elevated cancellations," driven by portfolio rationalization, clinical trial results, and the loss of a large COVID vaccine trial, as continuing headwinds.
  • Cutler cautioned that "the market and the environment continues to be volatile and continues to be a little uncertain."
  • Price competition is "probably intensified a little bit more... more recently," with management emphasizing the need to balance competitive pricing with margin protection.
  • Cancellations are expected to "continue in that sort of ballpark in the near term," and book-to-bill is projected to remain around current levels.

Final Takeaway

ICON management emphasized progress in Q2 2025 with stronger gross bookings, revenue ahead of expectations, and expanded partnerships in biotech and metabolic disease trials. While revenue guidance was raised to a new midpoint of $8 billion, the company maintains a cautious stance due to persistent volatility, elevated cancellations, and intensified price competition. Strategic advances in digital innovation and the launch of the Center for Obesity position ICON to capture growth opportunities, while disciplined capital deployment and continued investment in operational efficiencies remain priorities as the company navigates industry headwinds.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Plexus targets $100M in FY25 free cash flow and sequential revenue growth amid strong sector wins
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Plexus Corp. (PLXS) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Todd P. Kelsey emphasized Plexus' “ongoing strength in new program wins and opportunities to gain share in support of delivering growth outpacing our end markets.” Kelsey reported that Plexus grew revenue sequentially, matched its non-GAAP operating margin goal of 6%, generated better-than-expected free cash flow, reduced debt, and accelerated share repurchase activity. He stated, “We delivered non-GAAP operating margin of 6%, matching our stated goal. We once again generated better-than- expected free cash flow. And finally, we reduced our debt while accelerating our share repurchase activity and concurrently increasing our share repurchase authorization.”
  • On sector performance, Kelsey noted improved order activity from industrial and European customers, with early signs of increasing European defense sector activity offsetting the impact of evolving program ramp timelines and tariff uncertainties.
  • Plexus secured 41 new manufacturing programs for the quarter, with $250 million in annualized revenue potential when fully ramped, and reported a 7-year high in customer satisfaction.
  • CFO Patrick John Jermain stated, “Gross margin at 10.1% was slightly above the midpoint due to a favorable mix of service offerings and better fixed cost leverage... Non-GAAP diluted EPS of $1.90 exceeded our guidance due to the items mentioned and a favorable tax rate.”
  • Executive VP & COO Oliver K. Mihm highlighted that the “funnel of qualified manufacturing opportunities is up 4% sequentially, and robust at $3.6 billion,” with broad sectoral diversification and strong contributions from sustaining services.

Outlook

  • Plexus projects fiscal Q4 revenue between $1.025 billion and $1.065 billion, non-GAAP operating margin of 5.7% to 6.1%, and non-GAAP EPS of $1.82 to $1.97. Kelsey said, “At the midpoint, our fiscal fourth quarter would result in impressive non-GAAP EPS growth of 26% for fiscal 2025.”
  • The company now forecasts approximately $100 million of free cash flow for fiscal 2025 and expects “healthy year-over-year revenue growth from each of our market sectors without assuming end market demand improvement” for fiscal 2026.
  • Plexus anticipates sustaining its strong operating margin and free cash flow performance into fiscal 2026.

Financial Results

  • Q3 revenue was $1.018 billion, in line with guidance. Non-GAAP operating margin reached 6%, up 30 basis points sequentially. Non-GAAP EPS was $1.90.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $13.2 million. The company’s return on invested capital reached 14.1%, the highest in nearly four years.
  • Selling and administrative expense was $50 million, at the low end of guidance.
  • Plexus acquired 143,000 shares for $18.4 million in Q3. The buyback authorization was increased by $100 million.
  • Cash cycle at quarter end was 69 days, up one day from Q2. Capital spending guidance was reduced to $80 million to $100 million for fiscal 2025.

Q&A

  • David Williams, The Benchmark Company: Asked about semi cap demand pushouts and aerospace/defense strength. Mihm responded that pushouts in semicap are “idiosyncrasy specific to those programs” and not lost demand, while Kelsey stated, “We are seeing strong demand within our Defense and our Space subsectors, though, and particularly Defense. And... we’re starting to see signs of increased European defense demand.”
  • James Ricchiuti, Needham & Company: Queried about industrial business and Malaysia facility ramp. Kelsey acknowledged a forecast downgrade for semicap to “low double digits” growth, while Jermain noted the Malaysia facility will have “a pretty minimal drag in Q4,” expecting profitability “in a 4-quarter period.”
  • Jacob Moore, KeyBanc: Asked about capacity/utilization and engineering solutions. Mihm stated that full capacity could support “an excess of $5 billion” in revenue, while Kelsey described engineering as “in excess of $100 million,” with strong growth and diversification, and Jermain said margins are “double the manufacturing margin.”
  • Anja Soderstrom, Sidoti: Asked about Malaysia margins and tariffs. Kelsey said Malaysia sites “performed quite well.” On tariffs, Kelsey noted, “We passed tariff costs on to our customers. And we haven’t gotten pushback on that front.”
  • Additional questions covered tax legislation, healthcare growth confidence, and European defense drivers, with management citing new customer additions and leading indicators from engineering as sources of optimism.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral tone, focusing on demand variations in semicap, industrial, and healthcare segments, and probing for margin sustainability and impacts from tariffs and facility ramps.
  • Management’s sentiment was slightly positive, expressing confidence in “bullish” growth opportunities and sharing optimism about strong free cash flow and sectoral momentum, while acknowledging some headwinds. Kelsey used language such as “We are committed to creating long-term shareholder value” and “We’re bullish on the growth opportunities.”
  • Compared to last quarter, management’s tone remained confident, but with increased caution regarding semicap demand and continued focus on operational efficiency. Analysts consistently pressed for more detail on market-specific demand and the effects of macro headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal Q4 revenue increased slightly to $1.025 billion to $1.065 billion, versus the previous quarter’s Q3 guidance of $1.00 billion to $1.04 billion.
  • Non-GAAP operating margin guidance remained consistent at 5.7% to 6.1%. EPS guidance for Q4 was raised to $1.82 to $1.97 from Q3’s range of $1.65 to $1.80.
  • Management reiterated the free cash flow target for fiscal 2025 but lowered capital spending guidance.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from broad operational efficiency and working capital improvements to specific questions about sectoral demand, tariffs, and Malaysia facility ramp impact.
  • Management’s confidence in maintaining operating margin and free cash flow performance was unchanged, but there was more explicit discussion about sectoral challenges and program ramp timing.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited evolving program ramp timelines, tariff-related uncertainties, and modest end market demand as ongoing challenges.
  • Kelsey noted, “We anticipate delivering this revenue expansion through share gains, new program ramps and growth with new customers, overcoming modest end market demand, evolving new product ramp time lines and uncertainties created by tariffs.”
  • Analysts raised concerns about semicap demand pushouts, tariff impacts on customer behavior, and the initial margin drag from the new Malaysian facility. Management addressed these by emphasizing operational flexibility, passing through tariff costs, and the expectation of rapid profitability for new facilities.

Final Takeaway

Plexus management highlighted steady sequential revenue growth, strong operating margin achievement, and robust free cash flow, supported by diversified sector wins and new customer additions. The company raised its EPS outlook for the next quarter and reaffirmed a $100 million free cash flow forecast for fiscal 2025, while signaling continued growth into fiscal 2026 across all market sectors. Strategic investments in operational efficiency, engineering, and global capacity position Plexus to sustain financial performance and gain market share, despite program timing uncertainties and tariff-related headwinds.
